text: Using high-resolution mass spectrometry, we investigated the conditions under which charged microdroplets could induce dramatic acceleration of chemical and biochemical transformations relative to their bulk solutions. We probed two very different reaction scenarios, involving the covalent formation of bonds (Pomeranz–Fritsch synthesis of isoquinoline) and the noncovalent binding of a protein by its ligand (cytochrome c/maltose complex). For both cases, the acceleration factors with respect to the aqueous bulk solution were estimated to exceed one million and one thousand, respectively. Analyzing the impact of the fusion of microdroplets allows us to concentrate reactants in a very small volume (0.5 pl – 2 nl), while observations of the corresponding fused aqueous droplets suggest that little evaporation occurs during the corresponding time of flight. This therefore strongly suggests that the droplet–air interface is a critical component for facilitating the acceleration of the catalysed reactions, and that microdroplet chemistry is a very efficient general method for bypassing slow reaction kinetics barriers associated with slow or poor catalysed reactions. It should also provide a very new and unique environment to study the effect of molecular crowding, which is present in almost all biological systems.
